title: Avoid DataStore-wide TCP reconcile fan-out from TCP status updates
author: jds9090
body:
## Background

  We observed a repeated reconcile pattern around `TenantControlPlane` and `DataStore` updates.

  A `TenantControlPlane` status update can currently be observed by the `DataStore` controller. Once that happens, the `DataStore`
  reconciliation path can list all TCPs using the same DataStore and trigger reconciliation for every one of them. This means a status-only change on one TCP may fan out into reconciles for unrelated sibling TCPs that share the same DataStore.

  This behavior becomes risky when a TCP has another source of desired-vs-stored drift. Unsupported or non-defaulted Deployment fields can keep producing updates, which then refresh status timestamps and emit more TCP events. Those events can be amplified through the DataStore controller and re-trigger all TCPs using the same DataStore.

  ## Observed / suspected impact

  - One TCP status update can indirectly enqueue every TCP using the same DataStore.
  - A single drifting TCP may cause noisy or repeated reconciles for sibling TCPs.
  - The loop may continue as long as at least one reconcile produces a persisted status or resource update that emits another watch event.
  - In smaller environments, the loop may appear to stop if repeated `metav1.Time` status updates occur within the same second and therefore do not change the stored JSON value.
  - In larger or slower environments, events are more likely to cross second boundaries or overlap with other TCP updates, making the loop easier to sustain.

  ## Why this should be changed

  The DataStore controller has two different responsibilities mixed together:

  1. Reacting to actual DataStore-related changes that may require TCPs to be reconciled.
  2. Maintaining `DataStore.status.usedBy` based on which TCPs currently use the DataStore.

  The second responsibility is status bookkeeping. It should not trigger tenant control plane reconciliation, and it should not make a status-only TCP update fan out to all TCPs sharing the same DataStore.

  ## Desired direction

  Separate DataStore usage bookkeeping from DataStore-triggered TCP reconciliation.

  The safer behavior should be:

  - TCP create/delete or DataStore membership changes update `DataStore.status.usedBy`.
  - `DataStore.status.usedBy` is updated only when the calculated value actually changes.
  - TCP status-only changes do not cause DataStore-wide TCP reconcile fan-out.
  - The existing DataStore controller continues to react to real DataStore changes and related Secret changes.
  - DataStore deletion blocking can still directly inspect TCP usage, but this should remain independent from status bookkeeping.

title: ops: dead --controller-reconcile-timeout flag & unauthenticated metrics
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Two operational / configuration findings around the command entrypoints.

## Findings

### 1. `kubeconfig-generator` advertises `--controller-reconcile-timeout` but never applies it (Low)
`cmd/kubeconfig-generator/cmd.go:37,159`

`controllerReconcileTimeout` is declared and bound to `--controller-reconcile-timeout` (default 30s, help text promising it bounds external resource calls), but — unlike the manager, where it flows into `ReconcileTimeout` — the kubeconfig-generator never reads the variable. It is not wired into any reconciler (grep confirms only the declaration and the flag binding exist). Operators setting the flag get a silent no-op.

**Fix:** thread the value into the KubeconfigGenerator reconcilers' context/timeout, or remove the flag from this command.

### 2. Metrics endpoints served without TLS or authn/authz (Low, hardening)
`cmd/manager/cmd.go:114-116` (`:8080`) and `cmd/kubeconfig-generator/cmd.go:72-74` (`:8090`)

`metricsserver.Options` sets only `BindAddress`; no `SecureServing`, no `FilterProvider`. This is the stock controller-runtime default rather than a Kamaji-introduced regression, so it is framed as hardening. Prometheus metrics (TCP namespaces/names, datastore names/drivers, cert status, addresses) are served as plaintext HTTP on all pod interfaces with no auth — anyone with pod-network reach can scrape control-plane topology. Same area as monitoring discussion #1212.

**Fix:** enable controller-runtime secure metrics serving (`SecureServing: true` + `FilterProvider: filters.WithAuthenticationAndAuthorization`), or document that a NetworkPolicy is the intended control.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: utilities: map checksum collision & IPv6 host parsing
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Two shared-utility bugs in `internal/utilities/`.

## Findings

### 1. Map checksum concatenates values only — no keys, no separators → possible silent reconcile-skip (Medium)
`internal/utilities/checksum.go:71-103`

Both `calculateMapStringString` and `calculateMapStringByte`, after sorting keys, do:

```go
for _, key := range keys {
    checksum += data[key]   // value only — no key, no separator
}
```
So `{"a":"1","b":"23"}`, `{"a":"12","b":"3"}` and `{"a":"123"}` all hash identically — key identity and value boundaries are both lost. The sibling `CalculateStringSliceChecksum` (:43-56) explicitly writes a `{0}` separator with the comment *"Add separator to avoid collisions"*; the map variants never got that fix.

`CalculateMapChecksum` → `SetObjectChecksum` is the repo-wide change-detection mechanism (compared as `GetObjectChecksum != CalculateMapChecksum(Data)` across every cert/kubeconfig/kubeadm/datastore config Secret & ConfigMap). A collision means a genuine change hashes identical to the prior state and the reconciler **skips an update it should apply** — silent config/cert drift, no crash.

> Note: for the actual data (PEM certificates, kubeconfigs, binary secrets) the probability of a real collision is negligible, so real-world impact is low. But the code is objectively missing key+separator that the sibling function already demonstrates as intended, and the fix is trivial.

**Fix:** feed keys, a separator and values into the hash, mirroring `CalculateStringSliceChecksum`:
```go
h.Write([]byte(key)); h.Write([]byte{0}); h.Write(value); h.Write([]byte{0})
```

### 2. `GetControlPlaneAddressAndPortFromHostname` mis-parses IPv6 literals (Low)
`internal/utilities/ingress.go:11-25`

Splits on `":"` and treats `len(parts)==2` as host:port. IPv6 literals contain multiple colons (`2001:db8::1`, `[2001:db8::1]:6443`), so a raw IPv6 host yields `len(parts)>2` and the port branch is skipped, while `[addr]:port` puts `"[addr"` (mangled brackets) into the address. Latent correctness bug for IPv6 clusters.

**Fix:** use `net.SplitHostPort` with a fallback for the no-port case instead of `strings.Split(hostname, ":")`.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: resources: builder & addon wiring bugs (CoreDNS ImageTag, konnectivity ports)
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Resource-builder and addon-wiring bugs in `internal/resources/`. One user-visible (CoreDNS); the rest are latent (self-healing or currently unreachable via a caller/CEL guard) but worth hardening.

## Findings

### 1. CoreDNS custom `ImageTag` silently ignored — wrong guard field (Medium)
`internal/resources/addons/coredns.go:229-235`

```go
if len(tcp.Spec.Addons.CoreDNS.ImageRepository) > 0 {
    config.Parameters.CoreDNSOptions.Repository = tcp.Spec.Addons.CoreDNS.ImageRepository
}
if len(tcp.Spec.Addons.CoreDNS.ImageRepository) > 0 {   // copy-paste: should be ImageTag
    config.Parameters.CoreDNSOptions.Tag = tcp.Spec.Addons.CoreDNS.ImageTag
}
```
The sibling `kube_proxy.go:335` guards on `ImageTag` correctly. A user who sets `Addons.CoreDNS.ImageTag` but leaves `ImageRepository` empty gets their tag silently dropped — pinning the CoreDNS version via `ImageTag` alone does not work.

**Fix:** change the second guard to `if len(tcp.Spec.Addons.CoreDNS.ImageTag) > 0`.

### 2. `AdditionalPorts` transiently dropped when Konnectivity is enabled; fragile index-1 coupling (Low)
`internal/resources/k8s_service_resource.go:106-118` + `internal/resources/konnectivity/service_resource.go:46,115,150-156`

The main Service builder keeps index 0 (apiserver) and "index 1 iff name is konnectivity-server", then appends `AdditionalPorts`. Konnectivity `mutate` unconditionally writes its port to `Ports[1]`. On the reconcile where Konnectivity is first enabled, an existing `AdditionalPorts` entry is overwritten for that cycle (brief disruption), then self-heals next reconcile. Separately, `service_resource.go:46` guards `len(Ports) > 0` but reads `Ports[1]` (should be `> 1`) — safe only because `mutate` guarantees len≥2.

**Fix:** preserve/patch the konnectivity port by **name** (not hardcoded index 1) in both builders; change the guard to `> 1`.

### 3. Dead/misleading write guard for the etcd datastore server cert (Low)
`internal/resources/datastore/datastore_certificate.go:132-176`

For the etcd driver, crt/key are generated unconditionally, but the write of `server.crt`/`server.key` (173-176) is gated on `TLSConfig.ClientCertificate != nil` — a mismatch for the etcd path. Not exploitable today only because a CEL rule (`datastore_types.go:29-30`) makes `ClientCertificate` mandatory for `driver == etcd`. If that constraint were relaxed, etcd would generate a cert and silently fail to persist it → the validity check (122-128) fails every reconcile → perpetual regeneration churn.

**Fix:** write `server.crt`/`server.key` unconditionally in the etcd branch; keep the `ClientCertificate != nil` guard only for the SQL-driver branch.

### 4. Unconditional nil-pointer deref in `Agent.ShouldStatusBeUpdated` (Low)
`internal/resources/konnectivity/agent.go:53-57`

The third `||` clause dereferences `tcp.Spec.Addons.Konnectivity` unconditionally. Since `&&` binds tighter than `||`, `Konnectivity == nil` with empty status agent fields would panic. Currently unreachable — the only caller (`soot/controllers/konnectivity.go:57-59`) early-returns on `Konnectivity == nil` — but the function's own contract is unsafe for future callers.

**Fix:** prepend `tcp.Spec.Addons.Konnectivity != nil &&` to the third clause (or restructure with an early return).

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: webhook: admission validation gaps
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Admission-webhook validation gaps in `internal/webhook/handlers/` and `api/v1alpha1/`.

## Findings

### 1. `DataStoreOverrides` validation is dead code whenever `spec.dataStore` is set, and never runs on update (Medium)
`internal/webhook/handlers/tcp_datastore.go:25-51,65-79`

`checkDataStoreOverrides` is the only path that verifies each `DataStoreOverrides[].DataStore` exists and rejects duplicate `Resource` entries. In `OnCreate` it is only reached when `tcp.Spec.DataStore == ""` — the `if tcp.Spec.DataStore != "" { return nil, t.check(...) }` branch returns first. In `OnUpdate` it is never called. Because the mutating webhook (`tcp_defaults.go:81-83`) defaults `Spec.DataStore` to the configured `--datastore` flag, post-mutation `DataStore` is essentially always non-empty in real deployments → override validation is effectively dead. A TCP referencing a non-existent override datastore, or listing a duplicate resource, is admitted and only fails later at reconcile time.

**Fix:** always run `checkDataStoreOverrides` (in addition to the main check) in both `OnCreate` and `OnUpdate`, independent of whether `Spec.DataStore` is set.

### 2. Auto-defaulted DNS Service IP can fall outside its Service CIDR (Low)
`internal/webhook/handlers/tcp_defaults.go:43-57`

When `DNSServiceIPs` is empty, the default is computed as `ip[len(ip)-1] += 10` (IPv4) / `+= 16` (IPv6) on only the last byte of the network base. For a small CIDR (e.g. `10.0.0.0/29`) the result `10.0.0.10` is outside the subnet, which the validating handler `validateDNSServiceIPs` (`tcp_dns.go:76-88`) then rejects — so a valid TCP is denied by Kamaji's own defaulting. For a high last octet (e.g. `10.0.0.250/24`) `250+10` wraps to `10.0.0.4` (in-subnet but wrong address). Benign for the documented `10.96.0.0/16`.

**Fix:** compute the offset over the full address (`net/netip`) and validate the result lies within the CIDR, erroring clearly if the subnet is too small.

### 3. `appendEscapedString` does not escape JSON control characters below 0x20 (Low)
`api/v1alpha1/tenantcontrolplane_jsonpatch.go:66-83`

`JSONPatches.ToJSON` hand-rolls JSON serialization and escapes only `\ " \n \r \t`, emitting other bytes verbatim — including U+0000–U+0008, U+000B, U+000C, U+000E–U+001F, which are illegal unescaped in JSON strings. Quotes/backslashes are escaped, so this is not a structural-injection vector, but a raw control byte in a patch `Path`/`From` yields malformed JSON downstream. Constrained input space.

**Fix:** escape all runes < 0x20 as `\u00XX`, or delegate the string value to `encoding/json`.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: controllers: error, finalizer & status-reporting correctness
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Error-handling, concurrency and status-reporting correctness issues in the controllers.

## Findings

### 1. Finalizer-removal error swallowed during TCP deletion → stuck `Terminating` with no requeue (Medium)
`controllers/tenantcontrolplane_controller.go:215-225` (root cause `internal/resources/datastore/datastore_setup.go:166-180`)

The deletion path calls `resources.HandleDeletion(...)`, which returns whatever `Setup.Delete` returns. `Setup.Delete` wraps the `DatastoreFinalizer` removal in `RetryOnConflict` but on failure only logs (`logger.Error(...)`) and returns `nil`. A persistent non-conflict failure of the final `Client.Update` (API unavailable, webhook reject) removes nothing, is reported as success, and the reconcile ends with `ctrl.Result{}, nil` — no `RequeueAfter`. The TCP stays `Terminating` with the finalizer intact and no self-scheduled retry.

**Fix:** return the removal error from `Setup.Delete` so `HandleDeletion` propagates it and the reconciler requeues.

### 2. Soot manager goroutine writes the named return `err` → data race (Medium)
`controllers/soot/manager.go:243-249,385-407`

`Reconcile` has named returns `(res reconcile.Result, err error)`. The goroutine at :385 assigns that same `err` via `if err = mgr.Start(tcpCtx); err != nil`. On an immediate `mgr.Start` failure (bind error, cache-sync failure) this write races the parent's deferred read/return of `err`. Result: a race (under `-race`) and potentially a spurious parent error that fires `tcpCancelFn`, cancelling the manager it just launched.

**Fix:** use a goroutine-local variable, e.g. `startErr := mgr.Start(tcpCtx)`, instead of the shared named return.

### 3. Valid condition stamped with stale `ObservedGeneration` (Low)
`controllers/datastore_controller.go:244`

When setting `DataStoreConditionValidType` to True, `ObservedGeneration: ds.Status.ObservedGeneration` is used instead of `ds.Generation` (every other `SetStatusCondition` in this file uses `ds.Generation`). `Status.ObservedGeneration` is only assigned later in the deferred block (:124), so on the reconcile that first validates a new generation the condition records the previous generation. Observability-only.

**Fix:** use `ObservedGeneration: ds.Generation`.

### 4. Wrong error variable logged when fetching Kubernetes version (Low)
`cmd/manager/cmd.go:181-186`

```go
k8sVersion, versionErr := cmdutils.KubernetesVersion(...)
if versionErr != nil {
    setupLog.Error(err, "unable to get kubernetes version")  // logs `err` (nil here), not `versionErr`
}
```
On failure the operator logs a nil/empty error and silently falls back to `k8sVersion="Unknown"`; the real cause (RBAC, connectivity) is lost.

**Fix:** `setupLog.Error(versionErr, "unable to get kubernetes version")`.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: reconcile: panic-safety on malformed/empty kubeconfigs
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Three places index slices / dereference results without bounds/error checks, turning a malformed or mid-rotation kubeconfig Secret into a reconcile panic (crash-loop via controller-runtime `RecoverPanic`).

## Findings

### 1. `GetRESTClientConfig` indexes `Clusters[0]`/`AuthInfos[0]` without a length check (High)
`internal/utilities/tenant_client.go:62-68`

```go
CAData:   kubeconfig.Clusters[0].Cluster.CertificateAuthorityData,
CertData: kubeconfig.AuthInfos[0].AuthInfo.ClientCertificateData,
KeyData:  kubeconfig.AuthInfos[0].AuthInfo.ClientKeyData,
```
`DecodeKubeconfig` parses with `Strict:false` and can return a `Config` with empty `Clusters`/`AuthInfos`. An empty/truncated/hand-edited/mid-rotation admin kubeconfig Secret → index-out-of-range panic. This backs `GetTenantClient`/`GetTenantClientSet` used across controllers, so it crash-loops into the same poisoned state.

**Fix:** guard `len(Clusters)==0 || len(AuthInfos)==0` and return a descriptive error before indexing.

### 2. `NewCertAndKey` error unchecked → nil-deref panic / silent broken kubeconfig (Medium)
`controllers/kubeconfiggenerator_controller.go:305-317`

```go
clientCert, clientKey, err := pkiutil.NewCertAndKey(caCert, caKey, &clientCertConfig)

for name := range tmpl.AuthInfos {
    tmpl.AuthInfos[name].AuthInfo.ClientCertificateData = pkiutil.EncodeCertPEM(clientCert)  // clientCert may be nil
    tmpl.AuthInfos[name].AuthInfo.ClientKeyData, err = keyutil.MarshalPrivateKeyToPEM(clientKey)
    if err != nil { ... }   // checks the REASSIGNED err, not the signing error
}
```
The signing `err` is never checked. On failure `clientCert` is nil and `EncodeCertPEM(nil)` dereferences `cert.Raw` → panic. If `tmpl.AuthInfos` is empty the signing error is dropped entirely and a kubeconfig with no client credentials is persisted as success.

**Fix:** check `err` immediately after `NewCertAndKey` and return before using the results.

### 3. `certificate_lifecycle` reads `kc.AuthInfos[0]` without a length check (Low)
`controllers/certificate_lifecycle_controller.go:180`

`extractCertificateFromKubeconfig` unconditionally reads `kc.AuthInfos[0].AuthInfo.ClientCertificateData`. A structurally valid kubeconfig with an empty `AuthInfos` slice → index-out-of-range panic. Lower likelihood (needs a decodable but authinfo-less labeled Secret).

**Fix:** guard `len(kc.AuthInfos) == 0` before indexing.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: crypto: certificate & TLS hardening
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Certificate/TLS hardening findings in `internal/crypto/crypto.go` and `internal/datastore/datastore.go`. None is remotely exploitable on its own; these are least-privilege / robustness improvements.

## Findings

### 1. Client-auth certificate gets overly broad Extended Key Usages (Medium)
`internal/crypto/crypto.go:269-273`

`NewCertificateTemplate` (used for etcd client authentication, `datastore_certificate.go:143`) sets `ExtKeyUsage` to `ClientAuth`, `ServerAuth` **and** `CodeSigning`. For a pure client-auth certificate, `ServerAuth`/`CodeSigning` are unnecessary. The subject organization is also `system:masters` (line 264), i.e. a highly privileged etcd identity — if the key leaks it is additionally abusable as a server cert and for code signing.

**Fix:** restrict EKU to `x509.ExtKeyUsageClientAuth`.

### 2. Certificate serial number from `math/rand`; static `SubjectKeyId` (Low)
`internal/crypto/crypto.go:16,261,268`

```go
SerialNumber: big.NewInt(mathrand.Int63()),
SubjectKeyId: []byte{1, 2, 3, 4, 6},
```
`math/rand` is non-cryptographic and unseeded (deterministic per process start); `SubjectKeyId` is hardcoded for all certificates. The rest of the package correctly uses `crypto/rand`. Low risk (internal auth CA, signing itself uses `crypto/rand`), but violates RFC 5280's unique-serial recommendation.

**Fix:** generate the serial via `crypto/rand.Int(rand.Reader, limit)`; derive `SubjectKeyId` from the SHA-1 of the public key.

### 3. TLS client config without explicit `MinVersion` (Low)
`internal/datastore/datastore.go:51-53`

The constructed `tls.Config` sets only `RootCAs`/`Certificates`, no `MinVersion`. (No `InsecureSkipVerify` anywhere — good.) Impact is minimal since Go's client default floor is already TLS 1.2; purely defensive against future default changes.

**Fix:** set `MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12` explicitly.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: datastore: MySQL/PostgreSQL driver correctness & connection safety
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

Several correctness and resource-safety bugs in the relational datastore drivers (`internal/datastore/mysql.go`, `postgresql.go`). All were confirmed by reading the code.

## Findings

### 1. PostgreSQL connection options: pointer aliased instead of struct-copied (High)
`internal/datastore/postgresql.go:109-114`

```go
opt := &pg.Options{ ... }
fn := func(dbName string) *pg.DB {
    o := opt        // copies the POINTER, not the struct
    o.Database = dbName
    return pg.Connect(o)
}
```
`o := opt` aliases the shared `*pg.Options` that the root connection `pg.Connect(opt)` (line 117) also uses. Every `switchDatabaseFn` call mutates `opt.Database` globally. Under concurrent reconciles on the same PG datastore this is a data race on `opt.Database`, and `GrantPrivileges`/`DeleteDB`/`Migrate` can run against the wrong database.

**Fix:** dereference-copy the struct: `o := *opt; o.Database = dbName; return pg.Connect(&o)`.

### 2. MySQL migration switches to `namespace_name` instead of the configured schema (High)
`internal/datastore/mysql.go:62,84` (vs `50-51`)

`DBExists`/`CreateDB` use `tcp.Status.Storage.Setup.Schema`, but the `USE` statements build the DB name raw from `tcp.GetNamespace()`/`tcp.GetName()`:

```go
c.db.ExecContext(ctx, fmt.Sprintf("USE %s_%s", tcp.GetNamespace(), tcp.GetName()))
```
This does not match `GetDefaultDatastoreSchema()` = `normalizeNamespaceName()` which replaces `-`→`_`, and diverges entirely once a custom `DataStoreSchema` is set. For TCPs with a dash in namespace/name, or a custom schema, migration switches to a wrong/non-existent DB → silent data corruption/loss or migration failure.

**Fix:** use `tcp.Status.Storage.Setup.Schema` consistently for the `USE` statements.

### 3. `RegisterTLSConfig` uses a process-global constant key (High)
`internal/datastore/mysql.go:114-121`

```go
tlsKey := "mysql"
mysql.RegisterTLSConfig(tlsKey, config.TLSConfig)
```
The key is constant for all MySQL datastores, but `RegisterTLSConfig` writes into a process-global map. Two datastores with different CAs/client certs overwrite each other → a connection can validate against the wrong CA or present the wrong client certificate (last-write-wins / race).

**Fix:** use a per-datastore unique key (name/UID) and deregister after use.

### 4. `GrantPrivilegesExists` leaks a connection on the match path (Medium)
`internal/datastore/mysql.go:230-255`

```go
rows, err := c.db.Query(statementShowGrantsStatement) //nolint:sqlclosecheck
...
for rows.Next() {
    ...
    if grant == expected { return true, nil }  // returns without rows.Close()
}
```
No `defer rows.Close()`; the match path (the common success case) returns early leaving `rows` open. The `//nolint:sqlclosecheck` masks exactly this. Called on every reconcile (`datastore_setup.go:265`) → gradual connection-pool exhaustion.

**Fix:** add `defer rows.Close()` right after the error check and remove the nolint.

---
🤖 Reported as part of a Claude Code audit.

title: Tenant apiserver→kubelet authz breaks on k8s 1.36 kubeadm: kamaji does not create the kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client → system:kubelet-api-admin ClusterRoleBinding
author: lexfrei
body:
## Summary

Recent kubeadm cert hardening (cherry-picked into k8s v1.35.5 and v1.36.1) removed the `kubeadm:cluster-admins` Organization from the `apiserver-kubelet-client` certificate and instead creates a dedicated `kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client` → `system:kubelet-api-admin` ClusterRoleBinding. Kamaji provisions tenant control-plane RBAC by running a subset of kubeadm phases and does not create this binding, so a kamaji build vendoring the hardened kubeadm leaves the tenant apiserver without authorization to reach the kubelet API.

## Impact

On a tenant control plane created by a kamaji build vendoring k8s ≥ v1.36.1 (or ≥ v1.35.5), `kubectl logs`, `kubectl exec`, and `kubectl proxy` to pods fail with a `Forbidden` for `user=kube-apiserver-kubelet-client` on `nodes/proxy`. This affects every tenant cluster regardless of the tenant's own Kubernetes version.

## Root cause

- In `cmd/kubeadm/app/phases/certs/certlist.go`, `KubeadmCertKubeletClient` dropped its `Organization: [kubeadm:cluster-admins]` field. The field is present in v1.35.3 and v1.36.0, and removed in v1.35.5 and v1.36.1+ (cherry-picks of commit `c78bdf97ec96f612be86fcd955e67d1c5c817ace`, PR kubernetes/kubernetes#138957; umbrella kubernetes/kubeadm#2414).
- The same change adds `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI()` in `cmd/kubeadm/app/phases/bootstraptoken/node/tlsbootstrap.go`, creating ClusterRoleBinding `kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client` (subject: User `kube-apiserver-kubelet-client`) → ClusterRole `system:kubelet-api-admin`, wired into the init bootstraptoken phase.
- Kamaji's `internal/kubeadm/bootstraptoken.go` calls only `AllowBootstrapTokensToGetNodes`, `AllowBootstrapTokensToPostCSRs`, `AutoApproveNodeBootstrapTokens`, `AutoApproveNodeCertificateRotation`, and `clusterinfo.CreateClusterInfoRBACRules`. It never calls `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI` (0 occurrences of `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI` / `kubelet-api-admin` repo-wide).
- Kamaji does call `kubeconfig.EnsureAdminClusterRoleBinding` (the `kubeadm:cluster-admins` → `cluster-admin` path), which is why the admin kubeconfig keeps working while apiserver→kubelet does not.

## Reproduction

- kamaji vendoring k8s v1.35.3 (e.g. tag `26.3.5-edge`): the cert carries `O=kubeadm:cluster-admins`, kamaji creates the `kubeadm:cluster-admins` → `cluster-admin` binding, `nodes/proxy` works.
- kamaji vendoring k8s v1.36.2 (e.g. tag `26.6.4-edge`): the cert carries only the CN, no binding grants `nodes/proxy`, `kubectl logs/exec` returns Forbidden.

## Suggested fix

When provisioning tenant control-plane RBAC on vendored kubeadm versions that dropped the cert Organization, kamaji should run the equivalent of kubeadm's `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI` — create the `kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client` → `system:kubelet-api-admin` ClusterRoleBinding (subject User `kube-apiserver-kubelet-client`).

title: Expose allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts in TenantControlPlane ServiceSpec
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

`TenantControlPlane`'s `spec.controlPlane.service` (`ServiceSpec`) only exposes
`serviceType`, `additionalMetadata`, and `additionalPorts`. For a `LoadBalancer`
control plane the generated Service therefore inherits the Kubernetes default
`allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts: true`, so a NodePort (e.g. `30654`) is always
allocated on every node — with no way to turn it off via the TCP.

## Motivation

With Cilium + MetalLB (BGP), the API server should be reachable only via the
LoadBalancer VIP (and ClusterIP) on `6443`, not additionally via a NodePort on
every node. Keeping the NodePort is an unnecessary exposure of the control plane.
Today the only workaround is patching the Service out-of-band
(`kubectl patch ... allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts=false`) — which happens to
persist because the reconciler never sets that field, but it's fragile and
undocumented.

## Proposal

Add an optional `allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts *bool` to `ServiceSpec`. When set
and `serviceType: LoadBalancer`, propagate it to the generated Service's
`spec.allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts`. Default `nil` → current behaviour (`true`)
is preserved; no migration impact.

**Implementation note:** when set to `false` on a Service that already has a
NodePort assigned, the reconciler must also clear the port's `nodePort`, since
Kubernetes does not deallocate it automatically — and Kamaji's port loop
currently copies the live `nodePort`
(`internal/resources/k8s_service_resource.go:106-114`), so it would otherwise be
re-applied.

## Out of scope

Only `allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts`. Other Service knobs
(`externalTrafficPolicy`, `internalTrafficPolicy`, `sessionAffinity`) are
intentionally left out of this issue.

---
<sub>🤖 Issue prepared with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code).</sub>

title: Datastore etcd certificate rotation does not trigger kube-apiserver pod rollout
author: evgkrsk
body:
When the datastore CA secret is updated, Kamaji correctly regenerates the etcd client certificate for each TenantControlPlane and updates the `Storage.Certificate.Checksum` in the TCP status (`internal/resources/datastore/datastore_certificate.go:82`). The TenantControlPlane controller is triggered via `TenantControlPlaneTrigger` channel (`controllers/datastore_controller.go:147`) and re-enters reconciliation.

However, the Deployment's PodTemplate is never modified, so no rollout occurs:

- The PodTemplate annotation `storage.kamaji.clastix.io/config` references only `Status.Storage.Config.Checksum` (`internal/builders/controlplane/deployment.go:84`). The `Storage.Certificate.Checksum` is not included.
- The `templateLabels()` function (`internal/builders/controlplane/deployment.go:1150-1172`) includes MD5 hashes for all internal PKI certificate secrets (CA, API server, front-proxy, SA, kubeconfigs) but **not** for the datastore certificate secret (`tcp.Status.Storage.Certificate.SecretName`).

As a result, when the etcd mTLS certificate is rotated:
1. The new certificate is written into the tenant's `datastore-certificate` Secret.
2. Kubelet updates the projected volume files inside the running pod.
3. But **kube-apiserver does not reload** `--etcd-certfile`/`--etcd-keyfile` at runtime — it reads them only at startup.
4. The Deployment is not rolled out, so the pod continues with the old certificate.

Please correct me if I'm wrong anywhere.

**Version:** `b3068f8` (tag `26.6.4-edge-3-gb3068f8`).

**Workaround:** Manually trigger a rollout for each affected TenantControlPlane:

```sh
kubectl rollout restart deployment -n <namespace> <tcp-name>
```

**Related issues:** #1190 (same class of stale credentials after rotation), #1151, #975.

title: networkProfile.advertiseAddress accepts a hostname but silently breaks the API server (no validation + misleading docs)
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

`spec.networkProfile.advertiseAddress` is passed verbatim to the API server's
`--advertise-address` flag, which only accepts an **IP address**. However:

1. There is **no validation** (neither CEL `XValidation` on the CRD nor a webhook)
   rejecting a hostname in this field.
2. The **field documentation does not mention** that the value lands in
   `--advertise-address`, and even implies hostname-friendly uses
   (`ControlPlaneEndpoint`, `cluster-info`, `admin.conf`).

As a result, a user who sets a DNS name there gets a valid-looking resource that
is accepted by the API, but the tenant `kube-apiserver` enters `CrashLoopBackOff`
with an error that is three layers removed from the actual misconfiguration:

```
Error: invalid argument "testhcp.kubeapi.example.com" for "--advertise-address" flag: failed to parse IP: "testhcp.kubeapi.example.com"
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create a `TenantControlPlane` exposed via a `LoadBalancer` Service.
2. Set a DNS name instead of an IP:
   ```yaml
   spec:
     networkProfile:
       advertiseAddress: testhcp.kubeapi.example.com
   ```
3. The TCP is admitted without error.
4. The `kube-apiserver` container crashloops with the `failed to parse IP` error above.

## Root cause

`advertiseAddress` is forwarded unmodified to `--advertise-address`:

`internal/builders/controlplane/deployment.go` (~L672-695):
```go
// Use the advertiseAddress (tenant-facing VIP) for --advertise-address when set.
apiAdvertiseAddress := address
if adv := tenantControlPlane.Spec.NetworkProfile.AdvertiseAddress; adv != "" {
    apiAdvertiseAddress = adv
}
...
managed := map[string]string{
    "--advertise-address": apiAdvertiseAddress,
    ...
}
```

This is intentional and correct — `--advertise-address` populates the in-tenant
`kubernetes` Service endpoints and must be an IP. The same IP-only philosophy is
already documented for the LoadBalancer ingress address in
`api/v1alpha1/tenantcontrolplane_funcs.go` (`getLoadBalancerAddress`, which
explicitly rejects hostnames). The field was introduced in #1111.

The gap is that this hard IP requirement is neither **validated** nor **documented**
on the `advertiseAddress` field itself.

### Documentation mismatch

`api/v1alpha1/tenantcontrolplane_types.go` (~L34-38):
```go
// AdvertiseAddress is the address advertised to tenant-side consumers (workers, konnectivity).
// When set, the management address is used for CAPI and status reporting, while this address
// is used for kubeadm ControlPlaneEndpoint, cluster-info, and admin.conf.
// Both addresses are included in the API server certificate SANs.
AdvertiseAddress string `json:"advertiseAddress,omitempty"`
```

The doc lists only hostname-friendly consumers (`ControlPlaneEndpoint`,
`cluster-info`, `admin.conf`) and never states it is also the `--advertise-address`
value, nor that it must be an IP. A user reasonably concludes a DNS name is allowed.

## Proposed fix

Two complementary changes:

### 1. Validate that `advertiseAddress` is an IP (fail fast, clear message)

Add a CEL `XValidation` on the field, analogous to the existing `isCIDR` rules in
the same struct (`serviceCidr`, `podCidr`, `loadBalancerSourceRanges`):

```go
// AdvertiseAddress is the IP address advertised to tenant-side consumers ...
//+kubebuilder:validation:XValidation:rule="self == '' || isIP(self)",message="advertiseAddress must be a valid IP address"
AdvertiseAddress string `json:"advertiseAddress,omitempty"`
```

(Or, if CEL `isIP` is not available on the supported Kubernetes range, perform the
equivalent `net.ParseIP` check in a validating webhook handler, consistent with the
existing `tcp_*` handlers.)

### 2. Clarify the field documentation

Make the IP requirement and the `--advertise-address` usage explicit, and point
users who want a hostname to the supported pattern (DNS A-record → stable VIP IP,
hostname in `certSANs`):

```go
// AdvertiseAddress is the IP address (e.g. a stable VIP) advertised to tenant-side
// consumers (workers, konnectivity). It is passed to the API server's
// --advertise-address flag and used for the kubeadm ControlPlaneEndpoint,
// cluster-info, and admin.conf, and is added to the API server certificate SANs.
//
// This MUST be an IP address, not a DNS name (--advertise-address and the in-tenant
// "kubernetes" Service endpoints only accept IPs). To expose the control plane under
// a hostname, point a DNS record at a stable VIP and add the hostname to
// networkProfile.certSANs. For L7/hostname exposure see spec.controlPlane.ingress /
// spec.controlPlane.gateway (note the TLS-passthrough caveats for client-cert auth).
```

## Impact

- Low blast radius, but a poor first-run experience: misconfiguration is accepted
  and only surfaces as an API-server crashloop with an unrelated-looking error.
- The validation prevents the foot-gun at admission time; the doc fix prevents the
  misconfiguration in the first place.

## Environment

- Kamaji: `26.6.4-edge` (behavior present since #1111)
- Exposure: `LoadBalancer` Service (MetalLB)
- Kubernetes (tenant): v1.36.1

---
<sub>🤖 Issue prepared with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code).</sub>

title: kube-scheduler / kube-controller-manager readiness probe config is accepted but silently ignored
author: Jakob3xD
body:
## Summary

The `TenantControlPlane` CRD exposes `spec.controlPlane.deployment.probes.scheduler.readiness` and `spec.controlPlane.deployment.probes.controllerManager.readiness` (as well as the global `probes.readiness`). For **kube-scheduler** and **kube-controller-manager** these fields are validated and accepted by the API server but have **no effect**: no readiness probe is ever rendered on those containers, and the configured override values are never applied. Only **kube-apiserver** actually receives a readiness probe.

As a result, a `kubectl apply` that sets `scheduler.readiness` (or `controllerManager.readiness`) validates successfully and then silently does nothing — a configuration foot-gun, with no warning event or log.

## How it got here

PR #1086 ("expand configurable probes to all probe types") introduced the `Probes` configuration. Its commit message advertises *"liveness, readiness, and startup probes,"* and the generated CRD schema exposes a `readiness` block under `apiServer`, `controllerManager` **and** `scheduler` alike.

However, in the deployment builder the readiness wiring was only ever added for kube-apiserver. Scheduler and controller-manager got liveness + startup overrides only. The `readiness` field for these two components has **never had any effect since it was introduced** — it was not removed later, it was simply never wired up.

## Root cause

In `internal/builders/controlplane/deployment.go`:

- `buildKubeAPIServer()` creates a base `ReadinessProbe` (`GET /readyz`) and applies both `probes.Readiness` and `probes.APIServer.Readiness`.
- `buildScheduler()` and `buildControllerManager()` create only a `LivenessProbe` and a `StartupProbe`. There is **no `ReadinessProbe` object** on these containers, and the override block applies only `.Liveness` and `.Startup`:

```go
if probes.Scheduler != nil {
    applyProbeOverrides(podSpec.Containers[index].LivenessProbe, probes.Scheduler.Liveness)
    applyProbeOverrides(podSpec.Containers[index].StartupProbe,  probes.Scheduler.Startup)
    // probes.Scheduler.Readiness is never referenced
}
```

Note that `applyProbeOverrides` dereferences the probe pointer, so it cannot simply be pointed at the (nil) readiness probe — the fix must **create a base readiness probe first**, mirroring the apiserver path.

## Desired behaviour

`scheduler.readiness`, `controllerManager.readiness` and the global `probes.readiness` should render an actual readiness probe on the respective containers — consistent with kube-apiserver, with the CRD schema, and with the stated intent of #1086.

Suggested implementation (matching existing in-repo conventions):

- In `buildScheduler()` / `buildControllerManager()`, create a default `ReadinessProbe` using the same endpoint the existing liveness/startup probes already target: `GET /healthz` over HTTPS on the secure port (`10259` for scheduler, `10257` for controller-manager), with the same default values as the liveness probe.
- Then apply `probes.Readiness`, followed by `probes.Scheduler.Readiness` / `probes.ControllerManager.Readiness` as overrides — exactly like the apiserver block.

Wiring this up keeps all three control-plane components consistent and lets operators tune readiness alongside liveness/startup. (If maintainers instead consider a readiness probe unnecessary for these leader-elected, Service-less components, the alternative would be to drop the `readiness` field from the scheduler/CM CRD schema so it no longer silently no-ops — but consistency with the existing schema and #1086 argues for wiring it.)

## Related

- #1086 — introduced the configurable probes (readiness wired for kube-apiserver only).
- #1178 — touches the same `buildScheduler()` / `buildControllerManager()` probe code (startupProbe timing).

title: Soot manager keeps using stale admin kubeconfig after rotation by cert lifecycle controller
author: hrak
body:
Soot [starts a long-lived manager per TCP using the TCP's admin kubeconfig](https://github.com/clastix/kamaji/blob/5e576071f010baa587f8de9027b66324dcea7af5/controllers/soot/manager.go#L238), and [only tears it down on CA rotation or NotReady status of the TCP](https://github.com/clastix/kamaji/blob/5e576071f010baa587f8de9027b66324dcea7af5/controllers/soot/manager.go#L183).

The NotReady status of the TCP is set [when readyReplicas of the TCP deployment is equal to 0](https://github.com/clastix/kamaji/blob/5e576071f010baa587f8de9027b66324dcea7af5/internal/resources/k8s_deployment_resource.go#L159). With the default blue/green deployment strategy of maxSurge 100% and maxUnavailable 0, a running TCP deployment would never reach readyReplicas 0 during rollout under normal conditions, and thus would never reach NotReady status.

Now when a cert/kubeconfig rotation happens (without a CA rotation, so with the original CA), this causes the existing Soot manager to keep running using expired credentials until Kamaji is restarted.

A possible solution could be to include the admin kubeconfig checksum in the sootMap and  `case tcp.Status.KubeConfig.Admin.Checksum != "" && tcp.Status.KubeConfig.Admin.Checksum != v.kubeconfigChecksum` -> m.cleanup.

If this checks out i will gladly file a PR with a fix.

title: Openstack Implementation with CAPI doc results in error
author: sulochan
body:
## Summary

Following the OpenStack infrastructure provider guide:

https://kamaji.clastix.io/cluster-api/openstack-infra-provider/

I was able to successfully deploy the management/control-plane cluster running Kamaji. However, when applying the tenant cluster manifests as documented, the tenant control plane never reconciles and no tenant nodes are provisioned.

The `KamajiControlPlane` resource is rejected by validation with an error related to `spec.controlPlaneEndpoint`.

The documentation and examples do not clearly describe what value should be provided for `controlPlaneEndpoint` when using the OpenStack provider workflow, nor whether this field should be automatically populated by the Kamaji Control Plane Provider.

## Environment

### Kamaji

Version: latest
 
chartName: openstack-cloud-controller-manager
 Version: 2.30.1

chartName: kamaji
  version: 0.0.0+latest

Kubernetes: 1.33

### Cluster API Control Plane Provider Kamaji

clusterctl version
clusterctl version: &version.Info{Major:"1", Minor:"13", GitVersion:"v1.13.2", GitCommit:"ebd807c66351f3ad96ca99b68d2219305d794640", GitTreeState:"clean", BuildDate:"2026-05-13T13:09:07Z", GoVersion:"go1.25.10", Compiler:"gc", Platform:"linux/amd64"}
## API Resources

The installed CRDs expose:

```text
kubectl get crd | grep kamaji
kamajicontrolplanes.controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io            2026-06-20T15:44:32Z
kamajicontrolplanetemplates.controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io    2026-06-20T15:44:33Z
```

```
root@k8s-mgt:/home/ubuntu/kamaji/deploy# kubectl api-resources | grep kamaji
kamajicontrolplanes                 ktcp         controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2     true         KamajiControlPlane
kamajicontrolplanetemplates         ktcpt        controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2     true         KamajiControlPlaneTemplate
root@k8s-mgt:/home/ubuntu/kamaji/deploy# kubectl api-resources --api-group=controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io
NAME                           SHORTNAMES   APIVERSION                               NAMESPACED   KIND
kamajicontrolplanes            ktcp         controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2   true         KamajiControlPlane
kamajicontrolplanetemplates    ktcpt        controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2   true         KamajiControlPlaneTemplate
kubeadmcontrolplanes           kcp          controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta2    true         KubeadmControlPlane
kubeadmcontrolplanetemplates                controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta2    true         KubeadmControlPlaneTemplate
```

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Follow the OpenStack provider documentation:
   https://kamaji.clastix.io/cluster-api/openstack-infra-provider/

2. Deploy the management/control-plane cluster.

3. Apply the tenant cluster manifest as documented.

4. Observe the Kamaji controller logs.

## Expected Behavior

The tenant control plane should reconcile successfully.

Kamaji should create the tenant control plane components and provision the API endpoint according to the documented OpenStack integration workflow.

## Actual Behavior

The tenant control plane does not reconcile.

No tenant nodes are provisioned.

The controller reports:

```text
2026-06-21T09:27:33Z	ERROR	unable to update finalizers	{"controller": "kamajicontrolplane", "controllerGroup": "controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io", "controllerKind": "KamajiControlPlane", "KamajiControlPlane": {"name":"tenant-cluster-01","namespace":"kamaji-system"}, "namespace": "kamaji-system", "name": "tenant-cluster-01", "reconcileID": "6fd37ce6-5132-4081-85d9-8e3a8ee902f7", "error": "KamajiControlPlane.controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io \"tenant-cluster-01\" is invalid: spec.controlPlaneEndpoint: Invalid value: 0: spec.controlPlaneEndpoint in body should have at least 1 properties"}
github.com/clastix/cluster-api-control-plane-provider-kamaji/controllers.(*KamajiControlPlaneReconciler).Reconcile
	/workspace/controllers/kamajicontrolplane_controller.go:162
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).Reconcile
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:222
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).reconcileHandler
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:479
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).processNextWorkItem
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:438
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).Start.func1.1
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:313
2026-06-21T09:27:33Z	ERROR	Reconciler error	{"controller": "kamajicontrolplane", "controllerGroup": "controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io", "controllerKind": "KamajiControlPlane", "KamajiControlPlane": {"name":"tenant-cluster-01","namespace":"kamaji-system"}, "namespace": "kamaji-system", "name": "tenant-cluster-01", "reconcileID": "6fd37ce6-5132-4081-85d9-8e3a8ee902f7", "error": "KamajiControlPlane.controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io \"tenant-cluster-01\" is invalid: spec.controlPlaneEndpoint: Invalid value: 0: spec.controlPlaneEndpoint in body should have at least 1 properties"}
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).reconcileHandler
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:495
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).processNextWorkItem
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:438
sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/internal/controller.(*Controller[...]).Start.func1.1
	/go/pkg/mod/sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime@v0.23.3/pkg/internal/controller/controller.go:313
```

## Questions

1. For `controlplane.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha2`, what is the expected definition of `spec.controlPlaneEndpoint` when following the OpenStack provider workflow?

2. Should this field be:

   * populated by the user,
   * populated by CAPO,
   * or populated by the Kamaji Control Plane Provider after the Service/LoadBalancer is created?

4. Maybe documentation gap or something I am missing on my part?

title: Fix deprecated installCRDs flag in cert-manager chart installation.
author: frabru99
body:
### Description
There is a recurring documentation issue regarding the installation process of Kamaji on various environments. 
The flag `installCRDs=true` in installation of `cert-manager` helm chart result be deprecated in favor of  `crds.enabled=true` in versions above **v1.15.0**.  

### Affected Locations
Here is the list of files where the issue is present:
* [x] `docs/content/getting-started/kamaji-kind.md` - (Row 47) within *Installing Cert-Manager > Install Cert Manager using Helm*
* [x] `docs/content/getting-started/kamaji-generic.md` - (Row 67) within *Install Cert Manager*
* [x] `docs/content/getting-started/kamaji-aws.md` - (Row 143) within *Install Kamaji*
* [x] `docs/content/cluster-api/external-cluster.md` - (Row 152) within *Step 6: Install Kamaji and dependencies on the external cluster > Install cert-manager*
* [x] `docs/content/cluster-api/openstack-infra-provider.md` - (Row 611 - YAML Manifest) within *Apply add-on Helm release resources*


### Proposed Changes
1. Replace `installCRDs=true` with `crds.enabled=true` in the Helm installation commands and examples across the first four  mentioned locations.
1. Replace `installCRDs : true` with `crds.enabled : true` in the YAML Manifest in the last location affected.

title: fix: kube-scheduler and kube-controller-manager CrashLoopBackoff on every TCP creation delays cluster provisioning
author: mridulgain
body:
## Summary

Every `TenantControlPlane` creation experiences a race condition where `kube-scheduler` and `kube-controller-manager` crash on startup before `kube-apiserver` is ready. The resulting CrashLoopBackoff compounds exponentially and keeps the TCP in `Provisioning` for minutes, blocking all downstream provisioning (machines, workers, addons).

## Root Cause

Kamaji starts all 4 TCP pod containers simultaneously. Two of them — `kube-scheduler` and `kube-controller-manager` — race against `kube-apiserver` on startup. Both have `startupProbe.initialDelaySeconds: 0`, giving zero grace period.

**kube-scheduler** immediately syncs caches against `127.0.0.1:443`. When the apiserver isn't ready, the startup context is cancelled and the scheduler self-exits:
```
E Unable to sync caches — context canceled
E handlers are not fully synchronized — context canceled
I Requested to terminate, exiting
```

**kube-controller-manager** starts its HTTPS health server on `:10257` and attempts leader election. With `initialDelaySeconds: 0`, the startup probe fires before the health server is ready:
```
Startup probe failed: dial tcp ...:10257: connect: connection refused
Startup probe failed: context deadline exceeded
Container kube-controller-manager failed startup probe, will be restarted
```

Both trigger CrashLoopBackoff with compounding delays:

| Restart # | Backoff delay |
|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | 10s |
| 3 | 40s |
| 5 | 160s |
| 9+ | 5+ min |

The TCP can stay up to 6–8 minutes in `Provisioning` until 3/4 containers are fully `Ready`, blocking everything downstream. Observed TCP Ready times across multiple clusters without the fix:

| Cluster | TCP Ready |
|---------|-----------|
| tv5 | 5m41s |
| jun5-addon | 4m15s |
| verify-addon | 4m48s |
| audi | 8m25s |
| jeep | 3m58s |

## Verification

Create a new TCP and check number of restarts in the controlplane pods :

```bash
kubectl get pod -n <namespace> -l "kamaji.clastix.io/name=<tcp_name>" \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{range .status.containerStatuses[*]}  {.name}: {.restartCount} restarts{"\n"}{end}{end}'
# Expected: 0 restarts for kube-scheduler and kube-controller-manager
```

## Relevant Code

All references are in the `internal/builders/controlplane/deployment.go`:

- **`buildScheduler()`** (line 345) — sets `startupProbe.InitialDelaySeconds: 0` at lines 378 and 392
- **`buildControllerManager()`** (line 435) — sets `startupProbe.InitialDelaySeconds: 0` at lines 480 and 494

Both probes fire immediately with no grace period, racing against `kube-apiserver` startup.

## Proposed Fix

We propose increasing `startupProbe.initialDelaySeconds` from `0` to `20` for both `kube-scheduler` and `kube-controller-manager`. This gives each component a grace period before the probe fires, avoiding the race against `kube-apiserver` startup entirely.

Tested across multiple clusters, this eliminated CrashLoopBackoffs and brought TCP Ready time down from 4–8 minutes to consistently **under 2 minutes**:

| Cluster | TCP Ready (before) | TCP Ready (after) | Restarts (after) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| jun5-addon | 4m15s | 1m03s | 0 |
| t-rex | — | 2m27s | 0 |
| kube-race | — | 1m04s | 0 |
| kube-race-check | — | 1m34s | 0 |

Thanks!

title: Missing org.opencontainers.image Container Image Labels
author: s-diez
body:
The container image of Kamaji does not set any `org.opencontainers.image` labels, instead the labels are inherited by the base image.

```bash
docker inspect docker.io/clastix/kamaji:26.6.2-edge
...
   "Labels": {
                "dev.chainguard.image.title": "static",
                "dev.chainguard.package.main": "",
                "org.opencontainers.image.authors": "Chainguard Team https://www.chainguard.dev/",
                "org.opencontainers.image.created": "2026-04-23T11:01:57Z",
                "org.opencontainers.image.source": "https://github.com/chainguard-images/images/tree/main/images/static",
                "org.opencontainers.image.title": "static",
                "org.opencontainers.image.url": "https://images.chainguard.dev/directory/image/static/overview",
                "org.opencontainers.image.vendor": "Chainguard"
            }
```

These labels are used by some tools like [renovate](https://docs.renovatebot.com/modules/datasource/docker/#description) to get information like changelogs etc.

If you are interesed, I can provide a PR to set these Labels in the make target build.
Otherwise feel free to close this issue as these labels are only of informational nature and I only happend to notice it.

title: Missing ClusterRoleBinding kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client since 26.5.5-edge / k8s v1.36.1
author: s-diez
body:
## Summary

In Kubernetes v1.36.1 the group `kubeadm:cluster-admins` was dropped from the `apiserver-kubelet-client` certificate.
Instead a ClusterRoleBinding `kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client` is created in a new boostraptoken funktion in kubeadmin `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI`. The change was done in commit [b0b25e8](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/b0b25e8ade23a6066c28ad5c66151ec117b2808e).

The call of `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI` is missing in Kamajis of the kubeadmin function runBootstrapToken.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create a tenant cluster with Kamaji and Kubernetes v1.36.1.
2. Check if the ClusterRoleBinding exists: `kubectl get clusterrolebindings.rbac.authorization.k8s.io kubeadm:apiserver-kubelet-client`

## Results

### Kublet bootstrapping with the client certificate

I would guess with the Kamaji 26.5.5-edge and later, you cannot use the certificate from the secret `<tenant>-api-server-kubelet-client-certificate` to join nodes to the cluster. But we are using bootstrap tokens instead so this is unverified. And if the proposed change would fix this is equally unverified

### Authentication for monitoring with the client certificate

In the monitoring guide the example code suggests using the certificate from the secret `<tenant>-api-server-kubelet-client-certificate` to access the metrics endpoints of the controlplane pods. Starting with Kubernetes v1.36.1 this will no longer work. Even with the proposed change the user `kube-apiserver-kubelet-client` no longer has permissions to access the metrics endpoints. 

## Fix

I will create a PR shortly to call `AllowAPIServerToAccessKubeletAPI`.

I think the issue with monitoring permissions requires some discussion first on how you would like to handle it. 
Maybe a dedicated certificate with the group `system:monitoring` could be created.
